questions.q: A Service has `selector: {app: web, env: prod}`. Which pod will receive traffic from this Service?
options: A pod with `app: web` and `env: prod` and `version: v2`
A pod with `app: web` and `env: staging`
questions.explanation: Service selectors use AND logic — a pod must have ALL the specified labels to match. A pod with app:web, env:prod, version:v2 matches because it has at least those two labels.

questions.q: What command lists all pods that have the label `env=production` AND `tier=backend`?
options: kubectl get pods --label env=production AND tier=backend
questions.explanation: kubectl get pods -l env=production,tier=backend uses comma separation to express AND logic for equality-based selectors.

questions.q: What is the key difference between Labels and Annotations?
options: Labels support more characters than Annotations
Annotations can be used in selectors, Labels cannot
Labels are used to identify/select objects; Annotations store metadata not used for selection
Labels are visible only to humans; Annotations are read by Kubernetes controllers
questions.explanation: Labels are queryable and used for selection (Services, ReplicaSets, etc.). Annotations store arbitrary metadata (build IDs, docs URLs) that is not used in selectors.

questions.q: Which command removes the label `canary` from a pod named `my-pod`?
questions.explanation: Appending a hyphen (-) to a label key removes it: `kubectl label pod my-pod canary-`. This is a standard kubectl label management pattern.

questions.q: Which selector types support set-based expressions like `env in (production, staging)`?
questions.explanation: ReplicaSets and Deployments support set-based selectors (matchExpressions). Services and ReplicationControllers only support equality-based selectors.
